sources.name: Wikipedia — Comix Zone
sources.notes: Mechanics overview. Comic panel traversal, cell-to-cell navigation, paper plane weapon.

notes: Comix Zone is a beat-em-up by Sega Technical Institute with a unique comic book panel navigation system.
DEFINING MECHANIC: The game takes place INSIDE a comic book. The player moves between panels of the comic.
The 'level' is a comic book page. Each panel is a gameplay section. Transitioning between panels is part of the navigation.
UNIQUE HAZARD: Taking damage causes Sketch Turner (the player character) to bleed — the blood costs health AND is visible on the comic page.
Paper airplane: Sketch can tear a piece of the comic page and fold it into a paper airplane as a ranged weapon.
Extremely difficult — one of the hardest Genesis games. Only 6 episodes, no continues.
Walk and jump values not precisely documented from ROM — estimated from gameplay.

panel_system._note: THE DEFINING MECHANIC. The game world is organized as comic book panels on a page.
panel_system.description: Each 'level' is a comic book page containing multiple panels arranged in comic layout. The player navigates through panels by punching through panel borders or finding the direction of transition.

panel_transitions.standard: Walk or jump to the edge of the panel — transition happens automatically or after action
panel_transitions.punch_through: Punch through the panel border to break into the next panel faster
notes: Panel transitions are directional — the comic layout determines which direction leads where.
Some panels have multiple adjacent panels — choice of direction matters.
The panel system makes every room its own contained environment with defined boundaries.

panel_hazards.description: The comic page itself can be hazardous. Some panel borders have spiked or dangerous edges.
panel_hazards.notes: The meta-structure of the medium becomes gameplay. The panel border is both navigation tool and hazard.

jump.notes: Standard beat-em-up jump. Jump attacks are a key combat tool.

combat._note: Comix Zone is a beat-em-up — fist combat is the primary system.

punch.description: Sketch's primary attack. Punch combos deal progressive damage.
punch.notes: Punching through panel borders uses the same animation as punching enemies.

kick.description: Kick combo. Different hit area than punch.
kick.notes: Kick reaches different zones than punch — enemy heights matter.

super_move.description: Screen-clear special attack. Costs health to use.
super_move.notes: Emergency crowd clear at cost of HP. Same tradeoff as other beat-em-up supers.

paper_airplane._note: Unique ranged weapon — Sketch tears a piece of the comic page itself.
paper_airplane.description: Sketch rips a small piece of the comic page and folds it into a paper airplane projectile.
paper_airplane.cost: Uses a limited supply of paper from the current page — the page literally has less material after use
notes: Limited use per panel — the physical comic page is being consumed.
The paper airplane is slower than typical projectiles — enemies can potentially dodge it.
Thematically brilliant: the character is aware he's in a comic and uses the medium as a weapon.

rat_hatchet.description: Sketch has a small rat companion (Roadkill) and some level-specific items found in panels.
rat_hatchet.notes: Roadkill can be sent to attack or explore — limited use.

damage_visible.description: When Sketch takes damage, his wounds are shown as blood on the comic panel art.
notes: The damage visualization is meta — the comic page shows Sketch's injuries.
This creates a visceral presentation of damage that most games don't attempt.
It also reinforces the 'you are IN a comic' premise.

no_continues.description: Comix Zone has no continues. One run, no second chances.
no_continues.notes: Contributes heavily to the game's notorious difficulty. Six episodes with no safety net.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Comix Zone is the most explicit example of 'the player is inside the medium.' The comic panel system makes every rule of the medium into gameplay: panel borders are walls and navigation tools, the page material is a weapon resource, the comic's visual language (action lines, speech bubbles) is the game's visual language.

The paper airplane weapon is the most elegant meta-mechanic in the game — tearing a piece of the comic page to throw at enemies literally consumes the world to create a weapon. The player reduces the world's material substrate to gain a combat advantage. No other 16-bit game attempts this.

Comix Zone's no-continues design creates a high-stakes single-run experience — the entire game must be completed from start to finish without error. This was considered reasonable in 1995 (arcade cabinet legacy thinking) but was punishing for home players. It creates a sense of precious progress.

The beat-em-up genre in panel-by-panel navigation creates contained arena fights. Each panel is a self-contained encounter — you can't back out or run away once you've entered. The comic medium's naturally finite panel becomes a game design constraint: each panel is a fight you must win.

The blood-on-page health visualization is a design statement about violence in comics vs. games. Comics show violence as part of the medium; Comix Zone brings that presentation convention directly into the game. When Sketch bleeds, the art changes. Damage = artistic consequence.
